web.title: Claude Code can now take over your computer to complete tasks
web.description: An Anthropic video shows some examples of tasks Claud Code can complete on your desktop via Dispatch.
web.markdown:
[Skip to content](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/03/claude-code-can-now-take-over-your-computer-to-complete-tasks/#main)

Text
settings

Story text

SizeSmallStandardLargeWidth
\*StandardWideLinksStandardOrange

\\* Subscribers only

[Learn more](https://arstechnica.com/store/product/subscriptions/)

Minimize to nav


Anthropic is joining the increasingly crowded field of companies with AI agents that can take direct control of your local computer desktop. The company has [announced](https://claude.com/blog/dispatch-and-computer-use) that Claude Code (and its more casual user-oriented [Claude Cowork](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/01/anthropic-launches-cowork-a-claude-code-like-for-general-computing/)) can now “point, click, and navigate what’s on your screen” to “open files, use the browser, and run dev tools automatically” when necessary to complete tasks.

When possible, Anthropic says Claude Code and Cowork will still prioritize using [Connectors](https://claude.com/connectors) to directly access and control outside apps or data sources. When that connection isn’t available, though, those tools are now able to ask permission to “scroll, click to open, and explore as needed” on the machine itself to do what’s asked. This kind of direct control of the computer can also be initiated and managed remotely via [Claude’s Dispatch tool](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/13947068-assign-tasks-to-claude-from-anywhere-in-cowork) as long as the target computer remains powered on.

An Anthropic video shows some examples of tasks Claud Code can complete on your desktop via Dispatch.



The new feature is now available to Claude Pro and Max subscribers using MacOS in what Anthropic calls a “research preview.” That means the system “won’t always work perfectly” and will sometimes require a “second try” for complex tasks, Anthropic warns. Completing tasks via “computer use” also “takes much longer and is more error-prone” than performing the same task via Connectors, the company writes.

## Pobody’s nerfect

Giving an admittedly imperfect and “error-prone” AI tool the ability to explore your computer desktop “as needed” could ring some justified security alarm bells for many users. That’s especially true given the [widespread stories](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/02/the-rise-of-moltbook-suggests-viral-ai-prompts-may-be-the-next-big-security-threat/) of [security issues](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/02/openclaw-security-fears-lead-meta-other-ai-firms-to-restrict-its-use/) that [have](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-ai-alignment-director-openclaw-email-deletion-2026-2) [arisen](https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/aws/aws-service-outage-ai-bot-kiro) when companies or individual users give AI agents access to sensitive resources.

Anthropic says it has safeguards in place to prevent common risks like prompt injection, and it will limit access to certain “off limits” apps (e.g., “investment and trading platforms, cryptocurrency”) by default.

Anthropic also [notes on a support page](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/14128542-let-claude-use-your-computer-in-cowork) that the model is trained to avoid “risky operations” such as moving or investing money, modifying files, scraping facial images, or inputting “sensitive data.” But the company also warns that such training safeguards “aren’t perfect” and “aren’t absolute,” meaning that “Claude may occasionally act outside these boundaries.”

Anthropic also warns that, when computer use is activated, Claude will be able to see anything visible on-screen, including “personal data, sensitive documents, or private information.” For all these reasons, the company recommends “starting with the apps you trust and not working with sensitive data” during this research preview stage.

Anthropic’s announcement comes just weeks after the rollout of [Perplexity’s Personal Computer](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/03/perplexitys-personal-computer-brings-its-ai-agents-to-the-uh-personal-computer/), [Manus’s My Computer](https://manus.im/blog/manus-my-computer-desktop), and [Nvidia’s NemoClaw](https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/nvidia-announces-nemoclaw), which similarly let their AI agents take direct control of the desktop. All of these corporate moves follow [the viral spread of OpenClaw](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/01/viral-ai-assistant-moltbot-rapidly-gains-popularity-but-poses-security-risks/) earlier in the year, which led OpenAI to [hire OpenClaw creator Peter SteinBerger](https://x.com/sama/status/2023150230905159801) “to drive the next generation of personal agents.”

[![Photo of Kyle Orland](https://cdn.arstechnica.net/wp-content/uploads/2016/05/k.orland-13.jpg)](https://arstechnica.com/author/kyle-orland/)

[Kyle Orland](https://arstechnica.com/author/kyle-orland/) Senior Gaming Editor

[Kyle Orland](https://arstechnica.com/author/kyle-orland/) Senior Gaming Editor

Kyle Orland has been the Senior Gaming Editor at Ars Technica since 2012, writing primarily about the business, tech, and culture behind video games. He has journalism and computer science degrees from University of Maryland. He once [wrote a whole book about _Minesweeper_](https://bossfightbooks.com/collections/books/products/minesweeper-by-kyle-orland).

web.markdown:
Anthropic Update Lets You Control Claude Cowork With Your Phone

By [Ron Schmelzer](https://www.forbes.com/sites/ronschmelzer/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/ronschmelzer/")

Follow Author

[Innovation](https://www.forbes.com/innovation/)[AI](https://www.forbes.com/ai/)

# Anthropic Update Lets You Control Claude Cowork With Your Phone

By [Ron Schmelzer](https://www.forbes.com/sites/ronschmelzer/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/ronschmelzer/"),

Contributor.

Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
Ron Schmelzer covers AI and data best practices at Forbes since 2018

Follow Author

Mar 20, 2026, 01:02pm EDTMar 23, 2026, 02:36pm EDT

0

0:00 / 6:37

Claude Dispatch Lets You Control Claude Cowork With Your Phone

This voice experience is generated by AI. [Learn more](https://www.forbes.com/sites/forbesdigitalgroup/article/forbes-ai-audio-learn-more/).

This voice experience is generated by AI. [Learn more](https://www.forbes.com/sites/forbesdigitalgroup/article/forbes-ai-audio-learn-more/).

![In this photo illustration, the AI company Anthropic logo](https://imageio.forbes.com/specials-images/imageserve/69bd7d2b0f3c2816d5b88e31/In-this-photo-illustration--the-AI-company-Anthropic-logo/0x0.jpg?width=960)

SPAIN - 2026/02/04: In this photo illustration, the AI company Anthropic logo seen displayed on a smartphone with the Claude logo in the background. (Photo Illustration by Davide Bonaldo/SOPA Images/LightRocket via Getty Images)

SOPA Images/LightRocket via Getty Images

[Anthropic this week](https://x.com/felixrieseberg/status/2034005731457044577) introduced an update called Claude Dispatch that lets people control Cowork from a phone through what the company calls a persistent thread across desktop and mobile. Announced as a “research preview," as a new feature in Claude Cowork, Dispatch lets users kick off and monitor desktop tasks from a phone, pushing Claude closer to an operating layer for daily work rather than just a chat window.

The work still runs on the desktop computer and so local files stay there. The phone does not actually run or coordinate the activities, but it becomes the place where work gets assigned, checked and picked up again later. For most business users, AI still lives inside a familiar box. You open a browser, desktop app or terminal client, ask a question or perform a task and move on. Anthropic’s Cowork product has already been pushing beyond that model by giving Claude access to local files, browser tools and connected systems on a user’s own machine.

The mobile extension takes the next logical step. A user can start a task from a phone, let the desktop machine handle the file access and execution, then return to the result without sitting in front of the computer the whole time.

PROMOTED

Anthropic says the feature is rolling out to Pro and Max subscribers, with Max users getting it first and Pro following shortly after. The company’s support materials describe the experience as one ongoing thread with the same context available across devices. That means users are not starting over every time they switch screens. The conversation persists and so do the tasks.

## The Phone Becomes The Dispatch Layer

The agent market is already nudging users in the direction of mobile and messenger-first agentic AI interaction. OpenClaw, an open-source assistant built to work through WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Signal and a long list of other chat channels, treats messaging as the primary interface rather than a side feature.

MORE FOR YOU

### [‘Fortnite’ Financial Loss Leads To Over 1,000 Layoffs At Epic](http://www.forbes.com/sites/paultassi/2026/03/24/fortnite-financial-loss-leads-to-over-1000-layoffs-at-epic/ "‘Fortnite’ Financial Loss Leads To Over 1,000 Layoffs At Epic")

### [NYT Pips Today: Hints, Answers And Walkthrough For Tuesday, March 24](http://www.forbes.com/sites/erikkain/2026/03/23/nyt-pips-answers-tuesday-march-24/ "NYT Pips Today: Hints, Answers And Walkthrough For Tuesday, March 24")

### [Today’s NYT Strands Hints, Spangram, Answers: Tuesday, March 24 (Get Over It Or Get Through It)](http://www.forbes.com/sites/paultassi/2026/03/23/todays-nyt-strands-hints-spangram-answers-tuesday-march-24-get-over-it-or-get-through-it/ "Today’s NYT Strands Hints, Spangram, Answers: Tuesday, March 24 (Get Over It Or Get Through It)")

That matters because it reflects a wider change in user expectations. People increasingly want AI to behave less like software they visit and more like a capable operator they can text. Anthropic’s Cowork feature originates with Claude’s more web, desktop and terminal focus, but this newest addition shows that Anthropic is paying attention to the rapidly evolving agent market. The center of gravity is shifting toward messenger-first, mobile-friendly control.

In this case, Dispatch is not an attempt at an OpenClaw competitor. The existing locations for Claude operation remain, the files stay where they live and the configured tools sit where Claude can perform the tasks inside Cowork. The phone just becomes a lightweight command surface.

Anthropic’s documentation says users can assign tasks from the mobile app, let Claude continue working on the desktop and receive a finished output later. The examples are practical, including summarizing recent work, pulling together a brief, comparing materials across folders or producing a report while the user is away from the desk.

The goal is to remove small bits of drag that pile up over the day. The gap between “I need this done” and “I am at the machine that can do it” has always been one of those irritants. Workers compensate for it with delay, with workaround or with forgotten tasks. Anthropic’s update chips away at that gap.

Tools like Claude are no longer just a place to ask for text output or analysis. It is becoming a layer that can act inside an existing desktop environment, then report back through a mobile one. That makes the phone less of a productivity tool in its own right and more of a dispatch console for the tools that already exist elsewhere.

## Why Local Execution Is The Real Story

Rather than running and coordinating everything in cloud-based environments, Anthropic is betting that a local-first setup will appeal to users who want more capability at their desktop or in their networks without sending every sensitive document or workflow into a new external environment. For a bank, hospital, law firm or industrial company, the question is rarely whether AI can generate output. The real question is where the work runs, what it can touch and how much of the environment must be opened up to make it useful.

Anthropic makes it clear that phone-based control does not change that underlying model. That is an important design choice for companies dealing with internal documents, regulated data, proprietary research or ordinary corporate caution. While it does not eliminate risk, it narrows one source of concern by leaving execution anchored to the machine where the work already resides. This local-first posture may end up being one of the more commercially relevant aspects of the release.

Anthropic deserves some credit for being direct about the downsides of Cowork and Dispatch. Its support article warns that the feature can trigger actions on the user’s computer from the phone. Those actions may include reading, moving or deleting files, controlling the browser or interacting with tools and services that Cowork already has permission to use.

This is the trade that comes with every step from chatbot toward agent. The more useful the system becomes, the more consequential its access becomes. A model that can draft text is one thing. A model that can manipulate files, use authenticated tools and continue working when the user is not physically present is another.

Anthropic places limits around the experience. The computer must be awake. The Claude desktop app must remain open. Dispatch is a feature that is still labeled a research preview. Still, enterprise buyers will read this release through a security lens before they read it as a productivity story. Mobile convenience has a long history of expanding risk surfaces. The same pattern showed up in mobile banking, remote admin tools and cloud collaboration software. Claude Cowork may face the same test. The feature does not need to win over every company at once. It only needs to prove that the operational upside is strong enough for a defined class of users.

metadata.ogDescription: Anthropic’s new Cowork Dispatch update lets users kick off and monitor desktop tasks from a phone, pushing Claude closer to an operating layer for daily work.

web.title: Claude Code and Cowork can now use your computer - Engadget
web.description: Anthropic announced today that its Claude Code and Claude Cowork tools are being updated to accomplish tasks using your computer.
web.markdown:
Advertisement

Advertisement

Advertisement

![](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/BF5mR_OeiTLrYjvW_jz4ow--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTEyNDI7aD02OTk-/https://s.yimg.com/uu/api/res/1.2/QJ1qfm._y9nDX9n5Y9VGTw--~B/aD0yMTYwO3c9Mzg0MDthcHBpZD15dGFjaHlvbg--/https://d29szjachogqwa.cloudfront.net/images/user-uploaded/cowork-dispatch-blog-permissions.png)

Anthropic

Anthropic announced today that its Claude Code and Claude Cowork tools are being updated to accomplish tasks using your computer. The latest update will see these AI resources become capable of opening files, using the browser and running dev tools.

When enabled, the Claude AI chatbot will first prioritize connectors to supported services such as the Google workplace suite or Slack, but if a connector isn't available, it will be able to still execute an assigned task. Claude should ask for permission before taking these actions, but Anthropic still recommended not using this feature to handle sensitive information as a precaution.

Claude computer use will initially be available to Claude Pro and Claude Max subscribers on macOS. This feature is still in a research preview, so will continue to be adjusted based on Anthropic's user feedback. It will also support use with Anthropic's Dispatch feature, which allows a person to message the chatbot in a single continuous conversation across phone and desktop.

[Claude Cowork](https://www.engadget.com/ai/anthropic-launches-claude-cowork-a-version-of-its-coding-ai-for-regular-people-193000849.html) was introduced in January. It's an iteration of the [Claude Code AI agent](https://www.engadget.com/ai/anthropics-new-claude-model-can-think-both-fast-and-slow-203307140.html) for programmers that is designed for more casual users.
